---
title: "The Number Line"
subtitle: "Every number has a place; every place names a number"
lesson-id: "t1.number-line"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.what-is-a-number"]
unlocks: ["t1.add-subtract", "t1.negative-numbers"]
applied-domains: ["research", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Are you ready for this lesson?

1. Can you say the counting numbers in order from 1 to 20?
2. If asked "is 7 bigger or smaller than 4?", do you know the answer
   immediately?
3. Have you read [Lesson 1 — *What is a Number?*](../01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)
   and understood that a number is a quantity, not a symbol?

If you said no to (3), go back and read it first — the present lesson
genuinely depends on it.
:::

## Touch

Drag the slider. Watch the marker move along the line.

```{ojs}
viewof position = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "Pick a position:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 110, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -10, xMax = 10;
  const x = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const ticks = d3.range(xMin, xMax + 1);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`).attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");
  // axis
  svg.append("line")
    .attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad).attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // arrows
  svg.append("path")
    .attr("d", `M ${pad-2},${H/2} l 8,-5 l 0,10 z`).attr("fill", "#1f2933");
  svg.append("path")
    .attr("d", `M ${W-pad+2},${H/2} l -8,-5 l 0,10 z`).attr("fill", "#1f2933");
  // tick marks + labels
  for (const t of ticks) {
    svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", x(t)).attr("x2", x(t)).attr("y1", H/2 - 6).attr("y2", H/2 + 6)
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 1.4);
    svg.append("text")
      .attr("x", x(t)).attr("y", H/2 + 24)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13")
      .attr("fill", t === 0 ? "#c2185b" : "#1f2933")
      .attr("font-weight", t === 0 ? "bold" : "normal")
      .text(t);
  }
  // marker
  svg.append("circle")
    .attr("cx", x(position)).attr("cy", H/2).attr("r", 9)
    .attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text")
    .attr("x", x(position)).attr("y", H/2 - 16)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "14")
    .attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c")
    .text(position);
  return svg.node();
}
```

Move the marker right. The number gets bigger.
Move it left. The number gets smaller.
Pass through the red mark in the middle. Notice that the line **keeps going**
on both sides.

## Wonder

Sit with these for a moment. No need to write.

1. If you stand at position **5** and someone tells you to go to position
   **8**, which way do you walk?
2. What about from **5** to **2**?
3. There is no tick mark for **7.5** on the picture above. Could **7.5** still
   live somewhere on this line? Where exactly?
4. The line had numbers to the left of **0**. What kind of number is to the
   left of zero? When in real life would you ever need such a number?

## Discover

Work through these in order. Use the slider above when it helps.

### Problem 1

Place these numbers on a number line, in your head:
$$3, \quad 7, \quad 1, \quad 5, \quad 0$$

Now sweep your finger from left to right. In what order do you meet them?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Smaller numbers are to the left.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**0, 1, 3, 5, 7.** The number line gives you ordering for free — left to
right *is* smallest to largest. You did not have to reason; you just looked.
</details>

### Problem 2

Anu is at position **4**. Bina is at position **9**.

- Who is "ahead" if "right means more"?
- How many steps must Anu take (and in which direction) to catch up to Bina?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Move the slider above to 4. Now move it to 9. Count the ticks you crossed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Bina is ahead** (further right). Anu must take **5 steps to the right** to
reach her: $9 - 4 = 5$. The number line turns "how many more?" into "how far
along?".
</details>

### Problem 3

Look between the ticks for **3** and **4** on the line above. Is there
anything *between* them?

If someone asked you to point to **3.5**, where would you put your finger?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Between 3 and 4 is a gap. The gap has a middle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes — the line is solid, not a row of separate dots.** Between 3 and 4
there is a whole stretch of positions, and "3.5" is exactly the midpoint.

The ticks are just labels we drew. The line itself doesn't care where we put
ticks; it has a place for every number, including ones with halves and
tenths and hundredths in them. We will name those numbers properly in
**Lesson 7: Fractions** and **Lesson 8: Decimals**.
</details>

### Problem 4

A weather station reports today's temperature as **3 degrees**. Tomorrow's
forecast is "**5 degrees colder than today**". Where would tomorrow's
temperature sit on the line?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Start at 3. "Colder" means smaller — go left. Move 5 steps left.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
3, then 2, then 1, then 0... and now what? You went past zero. Keep going.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You land at **−2** — *negative* two — two steps to the *left* of zero.

The line keeps going past 0; we just write a small dash before the number
to mean "to the left of zero". These are called **negative numbers**, and
they get their own lesson — **Lesson 10**.

The fact that you can answer this question even though you have not formally
met negative numbers is the point. The number line *forces* the answer to
exist.
</details>

### Problem 5

Two friends sketch number lines.

- Tina's line marks every whole-number stop: $0, \ 1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 4, \ \ldots$
- Vimal's line only marks the even stops: $0, \ 2, \ 4, \ 6, \ 8, \ \ldots$

Are they both number lines? What is the difference?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Did Vimal *get rid* of the odd numbers, or did he just not draw them?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Both are number lines.** Vimal hasn't deleted the odd numbers — they are
still there in the line, between his ticks. He has just chosen a less
detailed labelling. The number line is one thing; tick marks are decorations
we add.

This is a quietly important idea. The mathematician's number line is the
underlying continuous line. The marks are bookkeeping.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **number line** is a straight line on which every number has a unique
position.

We pick one position and call it **0** (the **origin**). To its right are
numbers that grow without end: 1, 2, 3, 4, ... To its left are numbers that
shrink without end: −1, −2, −3, ... For any two numbers, the smaller one is
to the **left** of the larger one.

:::

The mnemonic, in the Bhāskara style:

> *Zero in the middle, growing right and shrinking left,*
> *Every number — whole or fraction, plus or minus —*
> *Has its single home on the unending line.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Order these numbers from smallest to largest, using the number line to
> reason: $\ 6, \ 2, \ 9, \ 4, \ 0$.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. Picture the line. Find the position for each number — at least roughly.
   - **0** is the origin.
   - **2, 4, 6, 9** are all to the right of 0, at increasing distances.
2. Sweep left-to-right. Read them off in the order I meet them.
3. The order I meet them is the order from smallest to largest.

**Answer:** $0, \ 2, \ 4, \ 6, \ 9$.

The point is *I did not compare them in pairs*. The line ordered them all at
once, just by where they sit.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Place these on the number line above (slide the marker to each in
turn): **8**, **−4**, **0**, **3**, **−1**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slide to 8 (eight ticks right of 0). Slide to −4 (four ticks left of 0).
Slide to 0 (the red mark). Slide to 3 (three ticks right). Slide to −1 (one
tick left). The marker physically traces the answer; nothing more to do.
</details>

**W2.** Which is larger: **−2** or **−7**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
On the line, "larger" means "to the right".
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**−2** is larger. It sits to the *right* of −7. Many adults guess −7
because "7 is bigger than 2", but on the number line the negative sign
flips which side you are on. (See *Common confusions* below.)
</details>

**W3.** Sketch a number line on a scrap of paper. Mark **0**, then **5**,
then **−5**. What do you notice about the distance from 0 to 5 and the
distance from 0 to −5?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The two distances are **the same** — five steps. Negative and positive
numbers are mirror images of each other across 0.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A frog starts at position **2**. It hops **3 to the right**, then
**5 to the left**, then **4 to the right**. Where does it land?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the slider. Track the frog one hop at a time.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
2 → 5 → 0 → 4. Read each step on the line.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 + 3 = 5$. $5 - 5 = 0$. $0 + 4 = 4$. **The frog lands at position 4.**
The number line lets you see the journey, not just the answer.
</details>

**P2.** True or false: between every pair of different numbers on the
number line, there is at least one more number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick two numbers — say 3 and 4. Is there a number between them? Now pick 3
and 3.5. Is there one between *those*?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**True.** Between 3 and 4 is 3.5. Between 3 and 3.5 is 3.25. Between any
two numbers, however close, you can always find one in the middle. The
number line is *dense* with numbers — there are no gaps.

This is one of the deepest facts about numbers. We will revisit it carefully
in **Tier 5** when we build the *real numbers* properly.
</details>

**P3.** Order these from smallest to largest using the number line:
$$-3, \ 5, \ 0, \ -1, \ 4, \ -8$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Bigger negatives are *further left*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-8, \ -3, \ -1, \ 0, \ 4, \ 5$. Read left to right. The −8 is the
furthest *left*, hence smallest.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Two friends both sketch a number line. Tina marks every whole
number: 0, 1, 2, 3, ... Mira marks only every tenth one: 0, 10, 20, 30, ...

If both lines are drawn the same length on the page, **whose line "shows"
more numbers between 0 and the right edge of the page?**

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is a trick question.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**They show the same numbers — they just *labelled* different ones.** Both
lines contain every number; the choice of tick marks doesn't add or remove
numbers. Mira's line covers a wider stretch (because her ticks are spaced
the same, but her labels jump by 10), but every number that lives on Tina's
line also lives on Mira's, and vice versa. The line itself is one continuous
object.

This idea — that the *scale* of a picture is independent of *what is
drawn* — comes back when we meet **graphs**, **coordinate systems**, and
eventually **functions** in Tiers 2 and 3.
</details>

**S2.** Imagine a number line stretching from your kitchen to the moon. Pick
any two real positions on it — say "the third floorboard in your kitchen"
and "the moon". How many numbers lie between them?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You established in P2 that between any two numbers there is another. Apply
that.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Infinitely many.** Between any two positions you can always find a
midpoint, and then a midpoint of *that*, and again, forever. Even between
two grains of dust on the kitchen floor there are infinitely many number-line
positions. This is one of the strangest and most beautiful facts in
mathematics. We will return to it carefully in **Tier 5: Real Analysis**.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"−7 is bigger than −2 because 7 is bigger than 2."** No. On the number
   line, −7 is *further to the left* than −2, so it is *smaller*. The minus
   sign flips which side of zero you are on, and "left = smaller" wins.
2. **"The number line stops at the edge of the page."** No. The arrows on
   the ends mean the line continues *forever* in both directions. The page
   is a window onto a much longer line.
3. **"Only whole numbers belong on the number line."** No. Every number —
   whole, fraction, decimal, even numbers we have not yet met — has a unique
   position. The whole-number ticks are a labelling convenience.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Can you explain to a friend why **−7 is smaller than −2**, using the
   word "left"?
2. Without scrolling up, can you state in one sentence what the number line
   *is*?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Lesson 3 — Place Value**.
- **2 yes:** redo *S1* and *S2*, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and pay particular attention to *Problem
  3* (the line is solid, not dots) and *Problem 4* (the line continues left
  of 0). These two ideas carry most of the weight.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 1 — *What is a Number?*](../01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)** —
  we needed to understand that numbers are quantities (not symbols) before
  we could ask where they "live".

### What this unlocks

- **Addition and Subtraction, Re-grounded** — every addition is "step
  right"; every subtraction is "step left". The number line *is* arithmetic
  in pictures. *(planned — see [Tier 1 index](../index.qmd))*
- **Negative Numbers** — we have already met them informally here; the next
  step is to operate on them. *(planned — see [Tier 1 index](../index.qmd))*
- **Place Value** — the number line shows that 13 is somewhere far to the
  right of 0, but the *digits* "1" and "3" don't live on the line at all;
  they are part of the *naming* system. *(planned — see [Tier 1 index](../index.qmd))*

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every coordinate axis in a game engine is a number
line. The position of a character along the floor is a number on the
horizontal line; the height of a jump is a number on the vertical line.
When we meet **vectors** in Tier 4, we are stacking number lines together.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** The number line is the picture behind the
**real numbers** — one of the central objects in modern mathematics.
Calculus, analysis, probability, and most of physics live on this line.
The 19th century saw it rebuilt from the ground up by Dedekind and Cantor;
we will retrace that journey in **Tier 5**.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Thermometers, rulers, clocks (time as a one-way line),
elevation maps, audio waveforms, even the address of a byte in computer
memory — all of these are number lines wearing different hats.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the slider widget makes the line
  literally a thing the learner moves on), Boole (the Wonder questions
  surface temperature, position, distance — intuitions the learner already
  has), Russian school (Problems 3 and 4 force the learner to *invent*
  fractions and negative numbers before we name them), Bhāskara (the
  *Name* beat is a single box plus a verse), 3Blue1Brown (the visual
  primacy: the line is the lesson; words follow).
- **No forward references** beyond the planned lessons listed in *Unlocks*,
  all of which appear in `_curriculum.yml`.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (visual line, verbal "step right/left", numerical examples) ✓, graduated
  hints on every exercise ✓, worked solutions ✓, common-confusions
  callout ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
